planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, rocky or loamy, lean to average soil in full sun to partial shade; European hophornbeam is remarkably drought tolerant and limestone-adapted, growing naturally on dry, rocky, calcareous soils in mountain forests of southern Europe where summer drought is common.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, rocky or loamy, lean to average soil in full sun to partial shade; European hophornbeam is a refined, small-to-medium deciduous tree of dry, rocky, calcareous mountain forests from southern Europe through Turkey and the Caucasus — closely related to the American hophornbeam (Ostrya virginiana) but slightly smaller and more drought-adapted. The distinctive fruiting clusters of papery, bladder-like inflated husks enclosing each nutlet hang in loose, pendulous clusters resembling hops (Humulus lupulus) — one of the most distinctive and ornamental fruiting features of any deciduous tree. The finely toothed, birch-like leaves turn gold in autumn. The wood is exceptionally hard and dense. More drought-tolerant than most other deciduous trees, making it an excellent choice for dry, alkaline, rocky garden soils in full sun or light shade. The hop-like fruiting clusters are attractive from summer through winter. An underused but beautiful ornamental tree for dry, chalky, or limestone-based soils where larger trees struggle.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average, deep loamy or clay-loam soil in full sun; northern catalpa is adaptable to a wide range of soils and tolerates both wet and dry conditions once established. Fast-growing — can put on 3–5 feet of height per year when young in moist, fertile conditions.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average, deep loamy or clay-loam soil in full sun; northern catalpa is one of the most bold and tropical-looking native trees of the central and eastern US — producing enormous, heart-shaped leaves (up to 12 inches long and wide), spectacular large clusters of white, orchid-like flowers with purple and yellow markings in early summer, and uniquely distinctive long, thin, bean-like seed pods ('Indian cigars' or 'catawba worms') up to 20 inches long that dangle from the tree through winter. The seed pods are entirely unique among North American trees. The catawba sphinx moth (Ceratomia catalpae) larva feeds exclusively on catalpa leaves and is so well-known as live fishing bait ('catalpa worms') that catalpa groves were historically planted near southern fishing ponds specifically to culture the caterpillars. The wood is exceptionally rot-resistant and was widely used for fence posts and railroad ties. A dramatic, fast-growing shade tree for large, open landscape areas.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep, loamy soil in full sun; Chinese toon grows very rapidly in moist, fertile conditions and can be kept as a large shrub by hard pruning (coppicing), which stimulates abundant new shoot production — the young shoots are the edible harvest. Coppice annually in late winter for a continuous supply of young, tender edible shoots.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep, loamy soil in full sun; Chinese toon is one of the most unusual food plants in world horticulture — a large, fast-growing deciduous tree whose young leaves and shoot tips are a prized traditional vegetable in Chinese cuisine ('xiang chun' in Mandarin), with a distinctive, complex, slightly onion-garlicky, savory, slightly resinous flavor unlike any other leaf vegetable. The young spring shoots — harvested when the leaves are emerging, brilliantly colored in red-bronze to burgundy — are stir-fried, used in tofu dishes, scrambled eggs, and pancakes, or eaten raw as a garnish. The harvest window is narrow (only the youngest, most tender growth is palatable; older leaves become tough and more bitter). Coppicing the tree in late winter each year keeps it shrub-sized (4–8 feet) and maximizes the production of young, tender shoots. One of the very few trees in the world cultivated specifically for its edible leaves. An extraordinary permaculture and forest garden plant for zones 6–9.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly alkaline to neutral or slightly acidic, fertile to average, deep loamy or clay-loam soil in full sun; silver linden is the most drought- and heat-tolerant of the lindens, native to southeastern Europe where summers are hot and dry. More suitable for urban conditions than other lindens.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly alkaline to neutral or slightly acidic, fertile to average, deep loamy or clay-loam soil in full sun; silver linden is distinguished from other lindens by the distinctive silver-white felt of dense, soft hairs on the undersides of the dark green leaves — when a breeze stirs the canopy, the alternating flash of dark green upper surfaces and brilliant silver-white undersides creates a shimmering, sparkling effect quite unlike any other tree. The heavily fragrant, creamy-white flowers in summer are powerfully honeyed and sweet — the flowers are harvested for linden flower tea (tilleul) across European countries, used as a relaxing, mildly sedative herbal remedy. The most heat- and drought-tolerant of the European lindens — surviving hot, dry summers in southeastern Europe better than other linden species. More adaptable to urban conditions (heat, air pollution, drought) than American basswood or littleleaf linden. An excellent street and shade tree where its large scale is appropriate. The silver leaf underside display when windblown is one of the finest effects of any ornamental tree.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep, loamy or sandy loam soil in full sun to partial shade; Chinese tulip poplar grows naturally in the moist subtropical and warm-temperate mountain forests of central and southern China, at elevations of 1,500–5,500 feet, in humid conditions with adequate summer rainfall.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep, loamy or sandy loam soil in full sun to partial shade; Chinese tulip poplar is the Asian counterpart of the American tulip poplar (Liriodendron tulipifera) — the only two species in the genus, separated by millions of miles of ocean yet producing very similar leaves and flowers. The leaves are distinctively more deeply notched (2 lobes per side rather than 1 in North American tulip poplar) and slightly smaller, giving a more intricate, finely cut appearance. The flowers are smaller and greener than the North American species' bold orange-and-yellow flowers, but share the same tulip-like form. Chinese tulip poplar grows more slowly and at a smaller ultimate size than American tulip poplar, making it more appropriate for gardens of moderate size. More tolerant of heat and humidity than North American tulip poplar in zones 7–9. Of great botanical interest as a 'living fossil' — a relict of the ancient flora of the Northern Hemisphere from the Cretaceous period, now restricted to two isolated populations on opposite sides of the Pacific Ocean.
